title: Into the Shadows of US Private Credit: A China Perspective
dek: From China, today’s US private credit expansion increasingly resembles pre-2017 Chinese shadow banking: rapid growth, weaker protections, and rising systemic interconnectedness.
keyFindings: US private credit growth mirrors several features of China’s pre-crackdown shadow banking era.
Interconnected exposures between private credit funds, banks, insurers, CLOs, and private wealth channels may amplify systemic risk.
China’s post-2017 regulatory discipline constrained shadow banking and improved conditions for institutional private credit with stronger downside protections.
As US deployment and exit pressures rise, global allocators may benefit from uncorrelated private credit opportunities in China.

paragraphs: As we observe the booming US private credit market from China, we see multiple parallels with China’s former shadow banking system, which expanded by trillions before Beijing’s 2017–2018 crackdown.

In contrast to China’s coordinated intervention, the US private credit market continues to expand while underwriting quality and liquidity dynamics appear to weaken. The result is growing concern that systemic vulnerabilities are compounding in less transparent channels.

Because US and China credit cycles often run counter to each other, these developments also underscore a broader allocator question: whether China’s regulated private credit ecosystem now offers a more attractive uncorrelated opportunity set.

sections.heading: Lengthening Shadows
paragraphs: After the global financial crisis, stricter bank regulation (including Volcker Rule and Basel III constraints) contributed to disintermediation and accelerated the rise of non-bank direct lending platforms in the US and Europe.

Hundreds of US direct lending funds have launched since 2008, but a large majority of managers have not operated through a full credit cycle. In a prolonged low-rate period, investor demand for yield intensified competition and compressed lender protections.

At the same time, private credit managers face substantial dry powder overhang, challenging deployment discipline while also reducing negotiating leverage for covenant quality and downside protections.

Exit dynamics have also weakened: lower distributions to paid-in capital, fewer M&A and IPO realizations, and heavier reliance on GP-led secondaries and continuation structures that can extend illiquidity without a fundamental business resolution.

sections.heading: Darkening Shadows
paragraphs: A higher-for-longer rate regime can pressure leveraged issuers through tighter interest coverage and weaker operating margins, especially in stagflationary conditions.
In this environment, payment-in-kind features, liability management exercises, and covenant flexibility may postpone formal defaults while increasing leverage and ultimate loss severity.
Valuation risk is also important: private credit marks are periodic and model-dependent, which can become fragile when cash-flow assumptions deteriorate and restructuring timelines stretch.
Even when defaults remain moderate in headline data, prolonged amendment cycles and repeat restructurings can leave portfolios with accumulating 'extend-and-pretend' risk.

sections.heading: Interconnectedness and Systemic Risk
paragraphs: The immediate liquidity profile of closed-end private credit funds can mask broader contagion channels through linkages with banks, insurers, securitization vehicles, and overlapping institutional LP bases.

In many leveraged capital structures, banks hold senior syndicated risk (often distributed through CLOs), while private credit vehicles hold junior or bespoke tranches. A single borrower shock can therefore transmit losses across multiple channels simultaneously.

As private credit platforms seek synthetic liquidity, they increasingly tap bank revolving facilities and term loans. At the same time, large managers are expanding into asset-based finance partnerships tied to bank-originated collateral pools.

The insurance nexus adds another channel: private capital ownership stakes, affiliated origination, and rising allocations to private credit and structured products can increase correlation during stress events.
A final concern is distribution to private wealth and retail channels as institutional fundraising slows, echoing patterns seen in China’s pre-crackdown era of shadow-bank product proliferation.

sections.heading: Lessons from China’s 2017–2018 Shadow Banking Clampdown
paragraphs: China moved proactively once off-balance-sheet leverage and retail transmission channels became systemically important, implementing unified oversight across major regulatory bodies.
Key measures included tighter rules for bank interactions with asset-management products, leverage limits, constraints on implicit guarantees, and cross-regulator reporting to the central bank.
From our perspective, that discipline helped curtail uncontrolled shadow intermediation and accelerated the migration toward more institutional, transparent private credit channels.
bullets: Restrictions on direct/indirect bank exposure to non-standard debt structures.
Prohibitions on implicit guarantees and regulatory arbitrage through trust structures.
Leverage caps and harmonized risk standards across similar product categories.
Mandatory cross-industry risk reporting to improve visibility of systemic linkages.

sections.heading: Regulatory Discipline and Opportunity
paragraphs: In private credit, more capital typically means lower yields and weaker terms. China’s tighter shadow-banking controls created the opposite setup: less undisciplined competing capital and stronger room to negotiate creditor protections.

In our view, institutional private credit in China now benefits from clearer approvals, enforceable first-lien structures, and legal recovery pathways that support multiple exit routes beyond public-market windows.
As global allocators diversify away from concentrated US exposures, contractual and collateral-based return streams in less-trafficked markets may become increasingly relevant.
Accordingly, we believe investors should monitor rising US private-credit interconnectedness while also evaluating uncorrelated opportunities in China’s regulated institutional private-credit market.

sections.heading: Sources Referenced in the Original Publication
paragraphs: People’s Bank of China; Financial Stability Board (Global Monitoring Report on Non-Bank Financial Intermediation 2024); SEC (2023); Federal Reserve and Federal Reserve Bank of Boston publications (2025); Moody’s (2025); S&P Global Ratings (2025); FEDS Notes, 'Bank Lending to Private Credit: Size, Characteristics, and Financial Stability Implications' (May 23, 2025); AM Best; Bank for International Settlements Annual Report (2024); Fitch; and other market commentary referenced by ShoreVest in the June 2025 white paper.
